A competitive regulatory mechanism of the Chd1 remodeler is integral to distorting nucleosomal DNA Received: 15 August 2024 Accepted: 9 April 2025 Published online: 28 May 2025 Check for updates

Ilana M. Nodelman Jean-Paul Armache

, Heather J. Folkwein 2,4, Wesley S. Glime1,3, & Gregory D. Bowman 1

1,4

The Chd1 chromatin remodeler repositions nucleosomes into evenly spaced arrays, a characteristic of most eukaryotic genes. Here we show that the yeast Chd1 remodeler requires two activating segments to distort nucleosomal DNA into an A-form-like conformation, a critical first step in nucleosome sliding. As shown by cryo-electron microscopy, these two activating segments together pack against the ATPase motor, where they are poised to stabilize the central ATPase cleft. These activating elements contact the ATPase at locations that are incompatible with binding of NegC, an autoinhibitory segment located between the two activators. NegC inhibits sliding by antagonizing the activators through steric competition and constraining activator placement, giving rise to directional nucleosome sliding. Given that activator reinforcement of the ATPase cleft is needed for DNA distortion, this first step in remodeling appears to provide a natural checkpoint for regulation of chromatin remodeler activity.

Chromatin remodelers are ATP-dependent enzymes that reorganize the nucleosome packaging of genomic DNA in all eukaryotes. Most remodelers have an ability to reposition the histone core on DNA, called nucleosome sliding, which is achieved through action of a conserved helicase-type ATPase motor1. Remodeler ATPases shift DNA around the histone core by coupling ATP binding and hydrolysis with distortions of the DNA duplex2–6. In nucleotide-free and ADP-bound states, ATPases can achieve an open conformation that under-twists DNA, producing a local A-form-like distortion. This distortion is the first step in repositioning nucleosomes, as it ratchets one DNA strand toward the ATPase by one nucleotide. Upon binding to ATP, the remodeler ATPase switches to a closed conformation that restores the twist of nucleosomal DNA and, in doing so, ratchets the duplex further into the nucleosome and eventually off the exit side2,7,8. The Chd1 remodeler, akin to most imitation switch (ISWI)-type remodelers, moves nucleosomes directionally, which is necessary for creating evenly spaced arrays 9–13 and resolving overlapping

dinucleosomes14. This directional sliding comes from an asymmetry in sliding rates, where the histone core is shifted faster toward one side than the other15. On end-positioned mononucleosomes, DNA flanking the nucleosome is only available on one side. Chd1 and most ISWI remodelers preferentially shift the histone core toward the flanking DNA, called entry DNA, which moves the core to a more central position (referred to as ‘centering’)15,16. Chd1 and ISWI remodelers have a DNA-binding domain C-terminal to the ATPase that binds to flanking DNA17–23. The remodeler ATPase can bind on either side of the nucleosome, with action on each side moving the histone core in opposite directions. A general question in the field has been how the location of the DNA-binding domain can bias the direction of nucleosome sliding. A challenge in studying the regulation of centering, however, is that remodeler reactions are dominated by sliding toward the preferred side, making it impossible to observe when inhibition slows or blocks sliding in the opposite direction. To better understand Chd1 regulation, a previous study modified nucleosomes

with a biotin–streptavidin block on one side, only permitting unidirectional sliding24. As shown with these biotin-blocked nucleosomes, wild-type Chd1 only shifted nucleosomes when its DNA-binding domain was bound to entry-side DNA. However, this requirement for binding entry-side DNA could be bypassed by disrupting autoinhibitory elements. One autoinhibitory element, named the bridge based on a crystal structure of the isolated Chd1 chromo-ATPase25, was needed to block nucleosome sliding when the DNA-binding domain was not on entry DNA. Functionally, the Chd1 bridge is similar to an inhibitory element called NegC for ISWI26, required for centering mononucleosomes27. The human ISWI remodeler Snf2h has a strong dependence on the nucleosome acidic patch for nucleosome sliding, which can also be bypassed by disrupting NegC28. Snf2h recognizes the acidic patch via a basic segment immediately N-terminal to the DNA-binding domain29 and analogous to entry DNA for yeast Chd1, Snf2h requires an intact acidic patch on the entry side for robust nucleosome sliding30. Based on these data, a general model has emerged that the NegC/bridge segments inhibit the ATPase motor unless the DNA-binding domain (for yeast Chd1) or acidic patch binding motif (human Snf2h) are bound on the entry side24,26,28,30. In crystal structures of the isolated remodelers, the NegC/bridge segments of Chd1 and ISWI pack against the ATPase motor in a similar fashion24,31 (Extended Data Fig. 1). In cryogenic electron microscopy (cryo-EM) structures where the remodeler ATPase is fully engaged with a nucleosome, the NegC/bridge region is disordered, indicating a dynamic nature. How the bridge/NegC segment controls nucleosome sliding activity has remained unclear. Structural comparisons of activated and autoinhibited chromatin remodelers have given hints of regulatory mechanisms on the nucleosome. Unlike most helicases, remodeler ATPases recognize duplex DNA, with the two DNA strands referred to as tracking and guide. The tracking strand corresponds to the DNA or RNA substrate common to all superfamily 1 (SF1) and SF2 helicases32, whereas the guide strand is the complementary strand, absent for helicases that act on single-stranded DNA. In the autoinhibited Chd1 crystal structure, the DNA-binding surface of the C-terminal RecA lobe (ATPase lobe 2) was blocked by two elements: the DNA tracking strand was blocked by an acidic linker of the chromodomains25, whereas the DNA guide strand was blocked by a helical segment on the N-terminal side of the bridge named the guide strand displaced (GSD) helix24. In autoinhibited ISWI structures, the tracking strand was blocked by an inhibitory element named AutoN31 and the guide strand by a helix analogous to the Chd1 GSD helix24 (Extended Data Fig. 1). The GSD helix therefore appears to be part of a common inhibitory mechanism, potentially working in concert with the chromodomains/AutoN24. For SWI/SNF remodelers, the ATPase motor appears to be stabilized by a helical segment called brace II, which packs against another helix called brace I on ATPase lobe 2 and protrusion 1 on ATPase lobe 1, thus extending contacts between lobes 1 and 2 at the central ATPase cleft33. Whereas the brace I helix, the C-terminal most helix of lobe 2, has been observed in all remodeler–nucleosome complexes, the brace II helix has only been observed for SWI/SNF and appears to be strongest in ADP-bound or nucleotide-free states, suggesting a nucleotide-dependent packing34. Here, we demonstrate that the C-terminal bridge of yeast Chd1 contains an inhibitory segment flanked on both sides by activating segments (Fig. 1). N-terminal to the inhibitor is the GSD helix, which has a complex role with an ability to disrupt nucleosome binding and, thus, be inhibitory while also being crucial for activation. As seen in our cryo-EM structure, the yeast Chd1 GSD helix packs against the central cleft between ATPase lobe 1 and 2, structurally analogous to brace II of SWI/SNF, and we therefore refer to it as the GSD/brace II helix. Our data suggest that both activators stabilize the central ATPase cleft in the open state, critical for DNA distortion. In opposition to the activators, the inhibitory segment—here named NegC, based on a similar decentering phenotype in ISWI—favors a closed state of the ATPase motor. Our

structure shows that NegC binding is incompatible with the activating position of the GSD/brace II helix and sterically clashes with the activator on the C-terminal side. We propose that the inhibitory and activating segments dynamically compete, with the activators favored when the C-terminal DNA-binding domain is on the entry side of the nucleosome.

Results

Directional sliding requires hydrophobic residues of NegC We investigated the ability of Chd1 variants to reposition precentered 40N40 nucleosomes. In native acrylamide gels, the nucleosomes migrate slower when the histone core is in the center of the DNA fragment and faster as the core shifts toward the DNA ends. To see what regions of the Chd1 bridge may affect sliding directionality, we generated a set of Chd1 variants where short segments were replaced by (Gly–Gly–Ser)n segments (flex) or individual hydrophobic residues replaced with Gly or Asn to increase flexibility or introduce polar character (Fig. 1a,b). In the decentering assay, most Chd1 variants produced four species that indicate repositioning of the histone core along the DNA fragment (Fig. 1c). As expected, wild-type Chd1 maintained central positioning of the histone core, as evidenced by the two slowest migrating nucleosome species that predominated. By contrast, most Chd1 variants produced altered distributions, with a notable increase in the fastest migrating species. To approximate the level of decentering, we quantified the fraction of the two fastest migrating species, as a measure of decentering (Fig. 1c, bottom, and Extended Data Fig. 2a). The most deregulated variants were those with residues 884–901 disrupted. In the autoinhibited crystal structure of Chd1 (ref. 25), this region packs as two short helices against lobe 2, contacting the ATPase with a cluster of hydrophobic residues that are conserved among several remodeler families (Extended Data Fig. 1). To see if the hydrophobic nature of these positions is important, we generated triple Leu→Asn and Leu→Gly mutants (L886N/L889N/L891N and L886G/L889G/L891G). Both mutants showed a similar decentering behavior as disruption of residues 884–889, indicating that the hydrophobic character of these positions is key for regulation. Based on the structure and impact of these substitutions, we consider these two amphipathic helices to encompass the core of NegC.

Two activator elements flank NegC Unlike other variants, Chd1[GSD/brace II]864-871-flex did not appear to shift 40N40 nucleosomes (Fig. 1c). To investigate further, we measured nucleosome sliding rates (Fig. 1d). In this assay, Chd1[GSD/brace II]864-871-flex was severely disrupted for nucleosome sliding, suggesting that it is critical for activity. However, when the disruption extended from 864 through the NegC region (Chd1[GSD/brace II+NegC]864-902-flex), a low but increased level of sliding activity was detected (Extended Data Fig. 2b). Therefore, the failure of Chd1[GSD/brace II]864-871-flex to slide was due in part to the presence of NegC interfering with nucleosome sliding. In addition to residues 864–871, other disruptions, both N-terminal and C-terminal to NegC, also reduced nucleosome sliding activity, including Chd1[activator]876-881-flex (>100 fold), Chd1[activator]F877N (>100-fold) and Chd1[activator]F917N/L918N/F921N (~20-fold) (Fig. 1c and Extended Data Fig. 2b). Thus, residues on both sides of NegC contribute to sliding activity, indicating an activating/repressing/activating arrangement.

The GSD/brace II helix antagonizes nucleosome binding In native gels, we noticed that Chd1[GSD/brace II]864-871-flex typically created a supershifted species that we suspected was a Chd1–nucleosome complex resistant to competitor DNA. To investigate this further, we measured nucleosome affinities for a number of Chd1 variants (Extended Data Fig. 3 and Supplementary Table 2). Compared with the wild type, Chd1 variants with a disrupted GSD/brace II helix (Chd1[GSD/ brace II]864-871-flex and Chd1[GSD/brace II]L865N/L869N) had remarkably higher affinities in nucleotide-free, ADP and AMP-PNP conditions. The higher affinity is consistent with the DNA-blocking position that

the GSD/brace II helix can occupy on ATPase lobe 2, where it would interfere with binding to the DNA guide strand (Extended Data Fig. 4a). As the GSD/brace II helix is followed by NegC and both concurrently bind ATPase lobe 2 in the autoinhibited crystal structure we wondered whether the ability of the GSD/brace II helix to block DNA binding may be a primary means by which NegC controls directional sliding. To test this possibility, we introduced a polar substitution (M652Q) to disrupt hydrophobic packing of the GSD/brace II helix against protrusion 2, a remodeler-specific insertion on ATPase lobe 2 (Extended Data Fig. 4b). The M652Q substitution increased nucleosome binding affinity in all nucleotide states, comparable with NegC disruptions but less than GSD/brace II helix disruptions (Extended Data Figs. 3c and 4c). This result is consistent with the idea that the M652Q substitution interferes with the inhibitory positioning of the GSD/brace II helix against protrusion 2. If the inhibitory position of GSD/brace II were a primary means for NegC inhibition, then the M652Q substitution should give a NegC phenotype. However, Chd1[M652Q] did not show substantially different decentering activity than the wild type (Extended Data Fig. 4d). Chd1[M652Q] also behaved similarly to the

wild type in the DNA distortion assay, as described below (Extended Data Fig. 2c). In the autoinhibited Chd1 crystal structure, the GSD/ brace II helix blocks DNA binding along with the chromodomains25. Combining the M652Q substitution with disruption of acidic residues on the chromodomains increased decentering compared with either mutation alone (Extended Data Fig. 4d), suggesting that both contribute to sensing flanking DNA. However, even this double disruption was less dramatic in decentering than the NegC disruption. Therefore, we conclude that while the GSD/brace II helix can antagonize nucleosome binding and contribute to inhibition, NegC must have another means of blocking ATPase activation.

NegC opposes and activators promote the ATPase open state We used a histone–DNA cross-linking assay to evaluate how the ability of Chd1 to distort DNA is affected by changes in and around the NegC region (Fig. 2). In this assay, cross-linking is achieved by labeling H2B(S53C) nucleosomes with azidophenacyl bromide (APB), which forms DNA cross-links 53 bp from the dyad upon ultraviolet (UV) irradiation that can be resolved into DNA nicks35. this assay is that Chd1 unwraps exit-side DNA19,20,36,37, and therefore, Chd1 molecules bound to the opposite face of the nucleosome reduce cross-linking efficiency as the DNA lifts away from the histone core. To prevent such interference, we blocked Chd1 binding to one side of the nucleosome by including a biotinylated nucleotide 19 bp from the dyad on the TA-poor side of the Widom 601 sequence. In the presence of streptavidin, this half-blocked nucleosome only allows Chd1 binding to the TA-rich side of the nucleosome19. We used an altered DNA sequence on the TA-rich side (called 601[TArich-rand2]), which produces a shift in H2B(S53C) cross-linking when Chd1 distorts DNA at superhelix location 2 (SHL2) (Fig. 2a) (ref. 2). Relative to the nucleosome alone, wild-type Chd1 shifted the H2B(S53C) cross-link by 1 nt in nucleotide-free and ADP-bound conditions (Fig. 2b, gray). This shift in the cross-linking site, which reflects Chd1’s ability to distort DNA at its SHL2 binding site, was improved with NegC variants and reduced with variants flanking NegC. The variants with impaired cross-linking shifts included disruptions of the activator regions: the GSD/brace II helix (residues 864–871, light green), residues 876–881 (dark green) and residues 917–921 (blue). These defects suggest that these activator regions normally help stabilize the ATPase motor in an open state on the nucleosome. By contrast, disruptions of NegC—spanning residues 884–907 (red/orange/yellow)—enhanced the cross-linking shift, suggesting that NegC antagonizes the open state. Given its central location between two activators, NegC could inhibit by interfering with activator function. A perplexing finding was that disruption of the GSD/brace II helix (Chd1[GSD/brace II]864-871-flex) endowed higher binding affinity for the nucleosome in nucleotide-free and ADP conditions (Extended Data Fig. 3), yet this variant failed to stabilize distorted DNA at SHL2 on the nucleosome (Fig. 2). The A-form-like DNA distortion requires binding of both ATPase lobes with nucleosomal DNA. To see if disruption of the GSD/brace II helix affected ATPase engagement with nucleosomal DNA, we introduced the single cysteine variant V721C to monitor binding of ATPase lobe 2 to nucleosomal DNA. Previously, we showed that V721C, labeled with APB, produced site-specific cross-links to the tracking strand of DNA, ~19 nt from the dyad2,19. Here, we observe that the GSD/ brace II disruption allowed for the same V721C cross-links to nucleosomal DNA as observed for the wild type but in more nucleotide conditions

(Extended Data Fig. 5). Consistent with previous results2, wild-type Chd1 cross-links to both sides of the nucleosome with ADP•BeF3−, yet only to the TA-rich side but not TA-poor side of the Widom 601 sequence with AMP-PNP. By contrast, the Chd1[GSD/brace II]864-871-flex variant robustly cross-linked to the TA-rich side in all nucleotide conditions and to the TA-poor side with AMP-PNP as well as ADP•BeF3−. This pattern reflects the more symmetric two-site binding observed for Chd1[GSD/ brace II]864-871-flex with the native gel binding assay, with two equally strong affinities in AMP-PNP, compared with the asymmetric strong/ weak affinities observed for the wild type (Extended Data Fig. 3). These cross-linking results confirm that tight binding observed for Chd1[GSD/ brace II]864-871-flex reflects binding of the ATPase motor to SHL2 on the nucleosome and that this interaction can occur in nucleotide-free and ADP conditions with a reduced propensity to distort nucleosomal DNA. Interestingly, in the H2B(S53C) cross-linking assay, DNA was distorted more in ADP than nucleotide-free conditions for a disruption that spanned both the GSD/brace II helix and NegC (Extended Data Fig. 2c). In nucleotide-free conditions, Chd1[GSD/brace II+NegC]864-902-flex was largely defective in the cross-linking shift, similar to Chd1[GSD/ brace II]864-871-flex and Chd1[activator]876-881-flex. However, in ADP conditions, it recovered a cross-linking shift approaching that of wild-type Chd1. This recovery in ADP conditions suggests that the poor cross-linking shifts for Chd1[GSD/brace II]864-871-flex and Chd1[activator]876-881-flex were due to inhibition by NegC. In nucleotide-free conditions, the lack of recovery suggests that the GSD/brace II helix and neighboring 876–881 region may directly stabilize an open state of the ATPase motor on the nucleosome.

Visualization of activators on the Chd1 ATPase motor Although these inhibitory and activating segments have been observed in Chd1 and ISWI crystal structures solved in the absence of nucleosomes (Extended Data Fig. 1b), they have not been visualized in remodeler–nucleosome complexes. We suspected that when bound to a nucleosome, these elements may be poorly ordered due to competition. To identify where the activating segments bind to the ATPase motor, we focused on a Chd1 variant with a disruption of the NegC core, Chd1[NegC]L886G/L889G/L891G. To favor an ATPase motor bound in an open conformation, we made a nucleosome substrate with the Widom variant 601[TArich+1] sequence, with an additional base pair

inserted between one pair of TA steps on the TA-rich side, which we previously used to capture a high-resolution cryo-EM structure of Chd1 distorting nucleosomal DNA in a nucleotide-free state3. As entry-side DNA is required for activation24, we generated an 80N0 nucleosome, such that flanking DNA would only be on the entry side of the nucleosome relative to the ATPase binding site with the +1 bp insertion. In the absence of nucleotide, this Chd1–nucleosome complex was applied to Quantifoil grids, frozen and collected on a Titan Krios system. From the initial >15.4 million particles, both 1:1 and 2:1 Chd1–nucleosome complexes were identified and refined by iterative rounds of 2D and 3D classifications and refinement. Combining these into a single dataset

yielded a final 2.37 Å resolution 3D reconstruction from 710,376 particles (Extended Data Figs. 6–8 and Table 1). As expected, for both the 1:1 and 2:1 complexes, the Chd1 ATPase was in an open conformation, with nucleosomal DNA distorted in an A-form-like conformation at the remodeler binding sites. In both complexes, we observed densities packed against the ATPase motor that corresponded to the activator segments flanking NegC (Fig. 3a,b and Extended Data Figs. 6 and 7). For the 2:1 complex, we initially observed a weak density for the DNA-binding domain on one side. Although these nucleosomes only have flanking DNA on the TA-rich side, the low occupancy for the DNA-binding domain and flanking DNA made it uncertain

[Page 6]

if all nucleosomes had the same orientation. Further classification and refinement focused on the DNA-binding domain revealed a unique orientation of the nucleosome, with density of purine–pyrimidine pairs that uniquely matched the 601 sequence and flanking DNA only on one side. For this 2:1 complex, an activator density was observed for both copies of the Chd1 ATPase, consistent with Chd1[NegC]L886G/L889G/L891G, achieving an activated state regardless of flanking DNA. Using multiple maps and combining particles into a highresolution combined map, we were able to build in the activator sequences (Fig. 3a,b and Extended Data Fig. 9). The GSD/brace II helix packs at the central cleft between the two ATPase lobes, where it contacts the brace I helix (residues 825–847) on lobe 2 and a remodeler-specific insertion called protrusion 1 (residues 560–591) on lobe 1. This positioning is similar to the brace II helix observed in a Snf2-nucleosome complex33. In the Chd1 structure, GSD/brace II density directly contacts the activator that is C-terminal to NegC, residues 914–922, which forms a helix and packs against brace I. These contacts suggest that these two activator segments may stabilize each other on the ATPase motor. To evaluate the importance of contacts to the activator segments, we tested Chd1 variants for nucleosome sliding and DNA-distortion abilities (Fig. 3d,e). Two residues that primarily contact activators are I838 and Y841, located on brace I. The side chain of Y841 stacks against F917, and loss of this aromatic side chain (Chd1[brace I]Y841A) impaired DNA distortion and reduced the sliding rate by approximately sevenfold. In the absence of the activators, I838 is largely solvent exposed, yet becomes completely buried by hydrophobic residues from GSD/ brace II and the C-terminal activator, making van der Waals contact with I868, F871, G872 and F917 (Extended Data Fig. 2d). Mutation of I838 to a polar residue (Chd1[brace I]I838N) resulted in a severe loss of sliding, consistent with disruption of activator binding. In addition to the hydrophobic contacts made with I838, the GSD/ brace II helix also projects two leucines, L865 and L869, against hydrophobic residues on protrusion 1 and brace I at the central ATPase cleft. A double substitution at these positions with asparagine (Chd1[GSD/ brace II]L865N/L869N) disrupted nucleosome sliding and DNA-distortion abilities similarly to the GSD/brace II] flex mutant (Extended Data Fig. 2b,c,e), indicating that they are critical for activator function. The activators are positioned such that they would be expected to help stabilize the central cleft of the ATPase. To investigate how much disruptions at the central cleft influence ATPase conformational stability, we separately introduced three hydrophobic-to-polar mutations, A842N (brace I, lobe 2), I843N (brace I, lobe 2) and I583N (protrusion 1, lobe 1), at the interface between the two ATPase lobes. The Cβ of A842 projects into a hydrophobic pocket lined by L865, I868, L869 of GSD/ brace II and I583 on protrusion 1. Introduction of a polar side chain at this position (Chd1[brace I]A842N), which would probably be incompatible with I583, gave a >50-fold reduction in sliding rate. I583 and I843 pack against each other, making direct contact across the central ATPase cleft. Activators compete with NegC for binding the ATPase motor The two activators are separated by NegC, which here was disrupted by Leu→Gly substitutions and not visible in the structure. However, the density for an intervening segment between the activators was visible, modeled as residues 894–899, suggesting that NegC would loop out over the C-terminal activator (Fig. 3b). After this crossover point of NegC, the density extending from the C-terminal activator continues along ATPase lobe 2. Two notable contact points are Y926, which packs against L699 and Q702, and W932, which wedges between two short helices called the major insertion site38 at the base of protrusion 2. These aromatic residues appear to anchor the activator against ATPase lobe 2. Following W932, the density continues over lobe 2 along the major insertion site as a helix (residues 938–954) that points toward entry DNA (Extended Data Fig. 9). As shown by superimposing the activator-bound structure with the autoinhibited crystal structure of Chd1, the inhibitory position of NegC is sterically incompatible with activator binding in two ways (Fig. 3f). First, the inhibitory position of NegC is too far to allow GSD/ brace II and the neighboring F877 to bind at their activating position seen in the structure. As shown in the autoinhibited structure, the NegC core starts in a helix that begins at residue A879. Being only two residues from F877, this short linkage would not allow the GSD/brace II helix to reach the central ATPase cleft seen in the active structure, which requires a shift of >16 Å. Second, the position of NegC in the crystal structure would sterically clash at multiple points with the C-terminal activator residues 914–931. Importantly, the main chain of the NegC core blocks the main chain of residues 928–930. This main chain clash, between Y926 and W932, occurs in the direct path of the activator (Fig. 3f). Moreover, the Y926 side chain overlaps with L889, L891 and V894 of NegC from the inhibited structure, further suggesting that these regions compete for ATPase binding. We tested the importance of these aromatic anchor residues with the variants Chd1[activator]Y926N and Chd1[activator]W932A. Previously, we showed that W932 is critical for nucleosome sliding activity39, and here, we show that both Chd1[activator]Y926N and Chd1[activator]W932A are deficient in both DNA distortion and nucleosome sliding (Fig. 3d,g). Based on the steric conflict with NegC, we suspected that the severity of these disruptions may have arisen in part from an inability to compete with NegC. In support of this idea, introducing the NegC disruption (884–889 flex) with each substitution for the aromatic anchors showed notable recovery of both DNA distortion and nucleosome sliding activities (Fig. 3d,g). We propose that this competition allows the DNA-binding domain to control remodeler activity (Fig. 5). In comparing the active and inhibited structures, there are three features that may contribute to a switch-like behavior, allowing Chd1 to respond to the location of the DNA-binding domain. First, the activating segment that directly competes with NegC ends in a helix (modeled as residues 938–954) that was poorly ordered and lower resolution (Extended Data Fig. 9e), suggesting a more dynamic nature. For yeast Chd1, this helix is separated from the DNA-binding domain by ~50 residues in a flexible linker39,40. The stability of this helix could be impacted by the location of the DNA-binding domain. Perched on the major insertion site of protrusion 2, this helix would be better accommodated with the DNA-binding domain on the more proximal entry DNA, compared with the more distal exit DNA, which is likely destabilizing. Second, a region of NegC, adjacent to the NegC core (modeled as residues 894–899), is bound to the ATPase in the activated state, alongside the NegC competitor (Fig. 3b). By its presence in the active state, this segment suggests that NegC can retain a foothold on lobe 2 even when outcompeted, which could allow a quick reversal. However, this NegC segment also appears to topologically constrain the protein chain in the activated state. As the C-terminal activator (residues 914–956) binds to lobe 2 underneath the disordered portion of NegC, conversion to the inhibited state would require release of this NegC segment. Such a topological reorganization may add a rate-limiting step. Third, the activating segment (residues 914–922) following NegC is positioned against lobe 1 in an autoinhibited state25. Discussion To slide the nucleosomes directionally, Chd1 uses its DNA-binding domain, C-terminal to the ATPase, to sense and respond to entry and exit DNA18,24. As previously shown, yeast Chd1 can only robustly shift nucleosomes when its DNA-binding domain is on entry-side DNA24. In this work, we describe a regulatory system (Fig. 5), which we propose couples the location of the DNA-binding domain with remodeler activity. This system regulates DNA distortion—a fundamental step in remodeling—and thus directly interfaces with the sliding mechanism. We identify the GSD/brace II helix as a dynamic structural element that can stabilize an A-form-like DNA distortion when it binds at the central ATPase cleft (Figs. 2 and 3). The activating location of the GSD/brace II helix at the central cleft, however, is incompatible with the NegC core binding to ATPase lobe 2 (Fig. 3f). For yeast Chd1, we define the NegC core as two amphipathic helices that bind lobe 2 through hydrophobic contacts that include L886, L889 and L891. GSD/brace II immediately precedes NegC in sequence, and with this short tether, NegC binding to lobe 2 precludes the GSD/brace II helix from reaching the central cleft. In addition to the segment containing GSD/brace II, NegC is also flanked on its C-terminal side by another activating segment (residues 914–956) (Fig. 3b, blue). The salient feature of this activator is its ability to directly compete with NegC. At one end (residues 914–921), this activator contacts brace I and GSD/brace II, and in the middle (926–931), it overlaps the binding site of the hydrophobic NegC core and thus appears to be a direct NegC competitor (Fig. 3f). In this competing region, this activator has two aromatic anchor residues, Y926 and

[Page 10]

precludes the aromatic anchors from reaching their docking sites on lobe 2. We noticed that disrupting residues 917–921 (Chd1[activator]F917N/L918N/F921N), while slowing remodeling, also decentered nucleosomes, suggesting that this region may also contribute to inhibition, which might be accomplished from its interactions with lobe 1. Based on structure and biochemistry, we conclude that the GSD/ brace II helix is a major contributor to stabilization of the central ATPase cleft. Yet, as shown in the autoinhibited crystal structure25, the GSD/ brace II helix can also occupy an inhibitory position on protrusion 2 that blocks lobe 2 engagement with DNA24. Given the increased nucleosome affinity of NegC mutants and the close physical linkage between GSD/ brace II and NegC, we suggest that the inhibitory position of the GSD/ brace II helix is probably stabilized by NegC. Thus, NegC can interfere with Chd1 action in two ways—by destabilizing the central ATPase cleft and by promoting a DNA-blocking position of the GSD/brace II helix (Fig. 5, inhibited). Our experiments with Chd1[protrusion 2]M652Q, designed to disrupt the inhibitory position of the GSD/brace II helix, did not show a strong decentering phenotype. Thus, although the GSD/brace II helix promotes dissociation from nucleosomes24, this inhibitory mechanism does not appear sufficient on its own to convey directional sliding. However, we did observe an increase in decentering by combining M652Q with disruption of chromodomain inhibition (Extended Data Fig. 4d). This result highlights how Chd1 possesses multiple layers of regulation that work together to tightly control activity. Given the similar structural and biochemical features shared between Chd1 and ISWI remodelers, we anticipate common regulatory principles for both families. We expect that similar to Chd1, ISWI has inhibitory and activating segments that dynamically compete and bias conformations of the ATPase motor. As ISWI, similar to Chd1, is sensitive to entry-side epitopes, a regulatory mechanism based on dynamic competition would probably be necessary to respond to the location of the DNA-binding domain and neighboring acidic patch binding motif. The unique requirements for maintaining an open conformation on duplex DNA may explain the Snf2-specific insertions found in remodeler-type ATPases. Unlike many other SF2-type ATPases, which act on single-stranded nucleic acids, remodeler-type ATPases are directly coupled to conformational changes in the DNA duplex. Accordingly, remodeler ATPases have reinforced the central cleft between the two lobes through Snf2-specific insertions—protrusion 1 extending from ATPase lobe 1 and brace I from ATPase lobe 2 (ref. 38). As shown in earlier work, disruption of this Snf2-specific interface is deleterious to nucleosome sliding activity for SWI/SNF remodelers31,33,41,42. Here, we find that similar disruptions with the Chd1[protrusion 1]I583N and Chd1[brace I]I843N variants not only eliminate nucleosome sliding but also destabilize the open conformation of the ATPase on the nucleosome (Fig. 3d,e). Interestingly, for nucleosomes made with 601[TArich+1] DNA, these disruptions also promoted cleft closure with AMP-PNP significantly more than the wild type (Fig. 4). Thus, in addition to cleft-stabilizing contacts reinforcing the open state, these contacts may also need to be weakened when progressing through normal ATP-dependent closure of the ATPase (Fig. 5, active) Outside of the CHD family, the central ATPase cleft appears to be the site of regulation for SWI/SNF and INO80 family remodelers as well. In SWI/SNF remodelers, protrusion 1 has been proposed to be part of a regulatory hub, as mutations in and around this region can either stimulate or interfere with ATPase action41,42. As shown by cryo-EM, protrusion 1 packs against two helical elements that are characteristic of SWI/SNF-type remodelers, called helicase-SANT associated (HSA) and post-HSA, which are also physically coupled to the actin-related protein (Arp)7/9 module34,43,44. Through the intimate interactions between protrusion 1, HSA and post-HSA domains, remodeler action has been proposed to be regulated by the position/orientation of the Arp7/9 module34. A similar architecture appears to regulate INO80-type remodelers as well, which contain HSA and post-HSA domains that link

https://doi.org/10.1038/s41594-025-01556-y

protrusion 1 to an Arp8 module45–47. In INO80, the Arp8 module directly contacts DNA and controls nucleosome sliding activity based on DNA geometry, presumably through its physical connection with protrusion 1 on the ATPase motor45,47–49. Given the common targeting of the central ATPase cleft, we speculate that the energetically expensive process of distorting duplex DNA is a likely point of regulation shared by all chromatin remodelers. The regulatory elements in chromatin remodelers imply that the expanded contacts between ATPase domains (protrusion 1 and brace I) define an energetically sensitive region. As different remodeler families all tap into this region, our results support the idea that an open, strained conformational state of the ATPase motor may be a common target for regulatory elements. These findings may point to a more general principle that regulation is easiest to achieve at the energetically most expensive steps. From an evolutionary perspective, a strained state is an ideal checkpoint where external cues could become required components for achieving an active configuration. For chromatin remodelers, we suggest that this checkpoint provides a common means for divergent inputs to control ATPase action. Thus, remodelers can be turned on and off by family-specific elements that recognize particular substrate features, producing unique remodeling outcomes, yet do so by tapping into the same stage of the ATPase cycle. Note added in proof: A recent study reported inhibitory and activating regions of yeast ISW1 that encompassed the NegC region50. A helix called the brake, analogous to the Chd1 GSD/brace II helix, was found to have an inhibitory but not stimulatory role.

Methods Proteins

All Chd1 variants described here spanned residues 118–1274 of Saccharomyces cerevisiae Chd1. Amino acid substitutions were introduced through primer mutagenesis (Supplementary Table 3) using NEB Phusion. His-tagged Chd1 proteins were expressed Escherichia coli in BL21Star (DE3) cells carrying an RIL plasmid for better codon expression. The Chd1 proteins were purified according to the following protocol51. The cell pellets resuspended in HisBind A buffer (50 mM Tris–HCl, pH 7.5, 500 mM NaCl, 10 mM imidazole (pH 8.0), 10% glycerol) were sonicated and spun at 4 °C, for 40 mins at 42,000g. The clarified lysate, passed over a glass fiber syringe filter, was loaded onto tandem 5 ml HisTrap HP columns and bumped off with buffer A + 175 mM imidazole. The pooled fractions were diluted fivefold to reduce NaCl to 100 mM, spun at 30,000g for 10 min and loaded onto tandem (5 ml) HiTrap SPFF columns. Chd1 was eluted in a linear salt gradient, and the pooled fractions were incubated overnight with Precission Protease to remove the His-tag. The next morning, imidazole (final concentration 7 mM) was added to the Chd1 sample, which was then repassed over a single (5 ml) HisTrap column. Flow through was concentrated to ~1 ml and loaded onto a HiLoad Sephadex S200 in 30 mM Tris–HCl, pH 7.5, 150 mM NaCl, 10% glycerol. The pooled fractions were aliquoted and flash frozen in liquid nitrogen and stored at −80 °C. Xenopus laevis histones were purified from inclusion bodies and reconstituted into octamers or dimers52. The histones were expressed in pLysS Rosetta cells with IPTG induction. The cells were lysed via sonication, centrifuged at 4 °C, 23,000g for 20 min. The inclusion body pellets were processed with two rounds of resuspension in histone wash buffer (50 mM Tris–HCl, pH 7.5, 1 mM EDTA, 100 mM NaCl) with 1% Triton X-100, followed by two rounds of resuspension in wash buffer minus detergent. Each resuspension step was followed by a 10 min, 23,000g spin at 4 °C. The final pellet was stored at −20 °C as a thin smear in a 50 ml conical tube. For histone purification, the histone smear was solubilized in DMSO followed by addition of 40 ml of unfolding buffer (7 M guanidine-HCl, 20 mM Tris–HCl, pH 7.5, 10 mM DTT). The conical tube was agitated for 1.25 h on an orbital shaker at room temperature followed by centrifugation at 23,000g at 18 °C for 15 mins. The supernatant was loaded in two ~20 ml injections onto a desalting column (Cytiva HiPrep 26/10) preequilibrated for each run in 7 M urea, 10 mM Tris–HCl, pH 7.5, 100 mM NaCl, 1 mM EDTA, 5 mM beta-mercaptoethanol. The peak fractions were then loaded onto three tandem HiTrap Q FF preceding three tandem HiTrap SP FF columns (Cytiva). Following the loading of the sample, the Q FF columns were removed, and the bound histone was eluted from SP FF columns via a linear salt gradient. The pooled fractions were dialyzed against milliQ water with 5 mM beta-mercaptoethanol and after three changes were aliquoted at 2 mg per tube and lyophilized and stored at −20 °C. To reconstitute into octamer or dimer, the purified histones were unfolded for 1 h at room temperature in unfolding buffer at a concentration of 2 mg ml−1. The histones were mixed together in a 1:1:1:1 (for octamer) or 1:1 (for dimer) molar ratio and diluted to 1 mg ml−1 in same buffer. The samples were dialyzed in three steps of 2 liters, 2 liters (overnight) and 1 liter refolding buffer (10 mM Tris–HCl, pH 7.5, 2 M NaCl, 1 mM EDTA, 5 mM beta-mercaptoethanol). The samples were concentrated to 1 ml and loaded onto a HiLoad Sephadex S200 (octamer) or a HiLoad Sephadex S75 (dimer) equilibrated in refolding buffer. The peak fractions were pooled based on assessment by 18% SDS–polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) and concentrated, and the sterile-filtered glycerol was added to a Cf = 10%. The purified histone octamer (or dimer) was aliquoted, flash frozen in liquid nitrogen and stored at −80 °C.

Nucleosome reconstitution and purification All the DNA fragments used in the nucleosome reconstitutions were prepared by PCR amplification followed by purification over a Bio-Rad Model 491 Prep Cell53. The peak fractions were pooled and concentrated,

https://doi.org/10.1038/s41594-025-01556-y

and the concentrations of purified DNA were determined by A260 absorbance. The nucleosomes were prepared by mixing DNA and histone octamer such that each was at a final concentration of 6 μM (ref. 52). To increase nucleosome yields, an additional 1.8 μM of purified histone dimer was added to the sample before dialysis. The nucleosomes were then formed via linear salt gradient dialysis. Each nucleosome sample was concentrated, loaded onto a 7% acrylamide gel column in the Bio-Rad Prep Cell Assembly and then electrophoresed at 10 W power53. The fractions were analyzed on 7% 60:1 acrylamide–Bis-acrylamide gels, and the desired fractions were pooled and concentrated. Glycerol was added to a final concentration of 20%, and the nucleosomes were aliquoted, flash frozen in liquid nitrogen and stored at −80 °C.

Decentering and centering native PAGE assays To test for decentering, 200 nM wild type or variant Chd1 was incubated with 20 nM (FAM)40-601-40 nucleosomes in 1× sliding buffer (15 mM Hepes–KOH, pH 7.5, 100 mM KCl, 5 mM MgCl2, 0.1 mg ml−1 BSA, 1 mM DTT, 5% sucrose). For centering assays, end-positioned 80-601-0(FAM) nucleosomes were used. The samples were preequilibrated at room temperature for 10 min, and then, 1 mM ATP was added to start remodeling reactions. For centering assays, at each time point, 1 μl of the Chd1 reaction mixture was transferred to quench buffer (15 mM Hepes–KOH, pH 7.6, 100 mM KCl, 0.1 mg ml−1 BSA, 1 mM DTT, 5% sucrose, 20 mM EDTA and 0.5–1.0 μg μl−1 salmon sperm DNA). For decentering assays, after 50 min, 10 μl of remodeling reaction was transferred to a fresh tube and 3.14 μl of 0.7 M glucose and 2 μl of 1 U μl−1 hexokinase were added. The samples were further incubated for 20 min, and then 1.3 μl, was transferred to 7.5 μl of quench buffer. The samples (3 μl per lane) were loaded on 5.5% 60:1 acrylamide:Bis-acrylamide native PAGE gels and electrophoresed at 100 V, 4 °C, for 2 h. The gels were scanned within the plates on a Typhoon 5 (Cytiva). The shifted and unshifted bands were quantified using ImageJ.

Binding titration experiments The binding titrations were performed in a series of individual tubes by incubating 2 nM (FAM)40-601-40 nucleosomes with either wild-type or variant Chd1 at final concentrations of 0, 2.44, 4.88, 9.77, 19.5, 39, 78, 156.25, 312.5, 625, 1,250, 2,500 and 5,000 nM. As Chd1 binds nonspecifically to DNA and forms nonspecific supershifted complexes with nucleosomes, we included salmon sperm DNA (1.0 mg ml−1 final) as a competitor. Inclusion of salmon sperm in the binding reactions is expected to reduce stability of Chd1–nucleosome complexes and therefore weaken the apparent binding affinity; however, this assay should still reveal relative differences in nucleosome binding for variants compared with wild-type Chd1. To set up the reactions, 10 μl of a master mix were transferred to each titration point tube such that the final concentrations of components were 2 nM nucleosome ± 1.0 mM nucleotide analog (hexokinase-treated ADP or AMPPNP), 1.0 mg ml−1 salmon sperm DNA and 1× buffer (15 mM Hepes–KOH, pH 7.6, 50 mM KCl, 2.5 mM MgCl2, 0.1 mg ml−1 BSA, 1 mM DTT, 5% sucrose). To bring the final volume up to 15 μl per tube, dilutions of Chd1 were added along with 1× buffer to achieve a 15 μl reaction volume. The tubes were incubated for 12–15 min at room temperature and then loaded on a 4.25% 60:1 acrylamide:Bis-acrylamide native PAGE gel. The gels were run in 0.25× TBE for 1 h in the cold room at 100 V and then scanned on a Typhoon 5. The bands corresponding to unbound, singly bound and doubly bound nucleosome species were quantified in ImageJ. The fraction bound was calculated by considering 2:1 complexes to be 100% bound, 1:1 complexes to be 50% bound (one bound SHL2 and one free SHL2) and nucleosome alone to be 0% bound. The fraction bound for each titration was fit in Python to two apparent dissociation constants (Kd1,app, Kd2,app) with the formula fraction bound = Amp × (0.5 ×

[Chd1] Kd1,app + [Chd1]

+ 0.5 ×

[Chd1] Kd2,app + [Chd1]

),

[Page 13]

where ‘Amp’ is the amplitude determined empirically to give the lowest residuals to the fits. The fits are given in Supplementary Table 2. For the constructs with the disrupted brace II helix, we suspected that the high affinity in ADP and nucleotide-free conditions reflected binding to the TA-rich side, since we previously found that the ATPase motor typically cross-linked more strongly to the TA-rich side2. To test this idea, we measured binding to 601[TArich+1] nucleosomes, which bind Chd1 more strongly in nucleotide-free conditions due to an additional bp on the TA-rich side3. Compared with the canonical 601 nucleosomes, the 601[TArich+1] nucleosomes with Chd1[864871]-flex produced binding isotherms that indicated tighter binding for the higher affinity site and similar binding for the lower affinity site (Extended Data Fig. 10). These results support the hypothesis that the higher affinity site observed in these experiments correlates with binding to the TA-rich side.

DNA distortion monitored by cross-linking The nucleosomes used in the DNA-distortion assay were all reconstituted with Xenopus histone H2A[wildtype], H2B[S53C] (SHL5), H3[C110A/M120C] (dyad) and H4[wildtype]. The nucleosomes were reconstituted with the following DNAs: Cy5-40-601-19-FAM, (Cy5)40[TArich + 1]601-19(FAM) or (Cy5)40-[TArich-rand2]601-19(FAM). The 40-[TArich-rand2]601-19 and 40-[TArich+1]601-19 nucleosomes had a biotin-moiety conjugated to a thymidine 19 nt from the dyad on the TA-poor side. These biotin-derivatized nucleosomes were incubated with streptavidin (Thermo/Pierce #21125) at a final concentration of 8 μM to block Chd1 binding on the TA-poor SHL2. The DNA-distortion reactions were performed similarly to previous work2. In brief, nucleosomes were prepared by first removing DTT via buffer exchange into 20 mM Tris–HCl, pH 7.5 and 5% glycerol. The nucleosome samples were then labeled in the dark by adding 1 μl of 20 mM 4-APB (ABCAM #ab144922; in 100% dimethyl formamide) to a final concentration of 250 μM APB and incubated at room temperature for 3 h. The labeling reactions were quenched with 5 mM DTT. The reaction mixtures (50 μl) containing 150 nM APB-labeled nucleosomes were incubated at room temperature with 600 nM Chd1 in the presence or absence of nucleotide analogs hexokinase-treated ADP (Cf = 2 mM), AMPPNP (Cf = 2 mM) or ADP•BeF3− (2 mM ADP, 15 mM NaF, 3 mM BeCl2, 6 mM MgCl2) and reaction buffer (20 mM Tris–HCl, pH 7.5, 50 mM KCl, 5 mM MgCl2, 0.1 mg ml−1 BSA, 5 mM DTT, 5% sucrose). After a 12–15-min incubation, the samples were irradiated at an UV wavelength of 302 nm for 1.25 min. Following irradiation, 100 μl of quench buffer (20 mM Tris–HCl, pH 7.5, 50 mM KCl, 0.1 mg ml−1 BSA, 5 mM DTT, 5 mM EDTA, pH 8.0) was added followed by 150 μl postirradiation buffer (20 mM Tris–HCl, pH 8.0, 0.2% SDS, 50 mM NaCl). The samples were incubated at 70 °C for 20 min, followed by addition of 300 μl phenol:chloroform:isoamyl alcohol (125:25:1, Sigma #P1944) and processed35. The samples were vortexed and spun in a microfuge for 2 min, 21,130g at room temperature. The sample was removed from the top layer leaving ~50–100 μl remaining. Next, a series of wash steps were performed such that 300 μl of 1 M Tris–HCl, pH 8.0, 1% SDS was added, and the samples were vortexed and spun in a microfuge (2 min, 21,130g, at room temperature), and the top layer was pipetted off again, leaving ~50–100 μl behind. These wash steps were repeated three more times, followed by the addition of 1 μl 10 mg ml−1 glycogen, 35 μl 3 M NaAcetate buffer (pH 5.2) and 750 μl 100% EtOH. The samples were vortexed and placed on ice overnight. The next morning, the DNA was pelleted by centrifugation at 21,130g, 4 °C, for 30 mins. The DNA pellets were then washed with 70% EtOH and centrifuged, and this step was repeated a second time. The DNA pellets were left to dry in the hood for 1 h, then resuspended in 100 μl of buffer containing 20 mM ammonium acetate, 0.1 mM EDTA, 2% SDS. The samples were vortexed and spun in a microfuge for 10 min, 21,130g, room temperature, and the supernatants were transferred to fresh tubes. For DNA cleavage, the samples were preincubated at 90 °C

https://doi.org/10.1038/s41594-025-01556-y

for 10 min, followed by addition of 5 μl of 1 N NaOH and a further 90 °C incubation. After 40 min heating at 90 °C, 105 μl of 20 mM Tris–HCl, pH 8.0, 4.5 μl 1 N HCl and 480 μl 100% EtOH was added to the samples. The samples were stored at −20 °C overnight. The next morning, the DNA was pelleted and washed twice in 70% EtOH, and the DNA pellets were left to dry. The DNA was resuspended in 4 μl of formamide and TBE loading buffer and loaded on 8% 8 M urea and 1× TBE denaturing gels. The gels were run on an S2 sequencing apparatus in 1× TBE at 65 W for 1.5–1.75 h and scanned on a Typhoon 5 scanner (Cytiva). The uncropped gels are shown in the Source data. Note that these chemically cleaved sample migrate 1–1.5 nt faster than the enzymatically generated DNA sequencing ladder.

Chd1-V721C cross-linking Chd1 cross-linking experiments were carried out according to an established protocol19. A single cysteine variant Chd1-V721C (7.5 μM) with a wild type or mutated GSD/brace II (864-871-flex) was labeled with 400 μM APB for 2.5 h at room temperature. The labeling was quenched when adding Chd1 (Cf = 600 nM) to reaction buffer (20 mM Tris–HCl, pH 7.5, 50 mM KCl, 5 mM MgCl2, 0.1 mg ml−1 BSA, 5 mM DTT, 5% sucrose) containing 150 nM (Cy5)40-601-19(FAM) nucleosomes and either nucleotide-free, 2 mM ADP (pretreated with hexokinase), 2 mM AMPPNP, ADP-BeF3− (2 mM ADP, 15 mM NaF, 3 mM BeCl2, 6 mM MgCl2). The samples were incubated for 15 min at room temperature, followed by UV irradiation for 1.25 min. The samples were processed following the UV step using the same protocol as in the ‘DNA distortion monitored by cross-linking’ section of the Methods.

Cryo-EM sample preparation The Chd1[L886G/L889L/L891G] variant (15.91 μM, 72 μl) and the 80N0 601[TArich+1] nucleosome variant (3.29 μM, 72 μl) were dialyzed against 20 mM HEPES pH 7.5, 100 mM KCl, 1.5 mM DTT, 2.5 mM MgCl2, 2.5% glycerol for 1 h in Slide-A-Lyzer buttons (3,500 Da molecular weight cutoff, 0.5 ml, Thermofisher). Following this, the remodeler and nucleosome were incubated together in a working ratio of 2:1 for 30 min at 4 °C. The complex was then concentrated using 100 kDa centrifugal filters (Amicon Ultracel, 0.5 ml, Millipore) to yield a final concentration of 3.0 mg ml−1. The sample was diluted to a concentration of 2.0 mg ml−1 before preparing cryo-EM grids. Quantifoil Cu 200 mesh, R 1.2/1.3 grids were glow discharged for 10 s at 15 mA in a Pelco easiGlow system. Next, 3.5 μl of the sample was applied to the grid surface, blotted for 3 s and plunged into liquid ethane maintained at liquid nitrogen temperature using the FEI Vitrobot IV maintained at 4 °C with 100% humidity. The gels of the purified materials used for cryo-EM are given in Supplementary Data File 1.

Cryo-EM data collection and analysis The grids were screened for ice quality and sample distribution on a TFS Talos Arctica equipped with a Falcon 4 detector at the Huck Institutes of the Life Sciences Cryo-Electron Microscopy Facility at the Pennsylvania State University. The data were subsequently collected at the Frederick National Laboratory for Cancer Research on an TFS Titan Krios system operated at 300 kV, in nanoprobe mode, equipped with a Gatan K3 camera. The images were collected as movies in super-resolution mode at nominal magnification of 81,000× with a physical pixel size of 1.0504 Å (super resolution of 0.5252 Å), resulting in 17,624 images, each fractionated into 40 frames accumulating 50 e− Å−2 (0.0825 s per frame, 1.25 e− Å−1 per frame) total dose. The data were refined using two consecutive approaches. The data were initially motion corrected using CryoSPARC54 ‘Patch Motion Correction’, classified and refined using ‘Hetero Refinement’ and ‘Non-Uniform Refinement’, respectively, and improved iteratively using ‘Reference-based Motion Correction’. This yielded a reconstruction with a Chd1 single-bound to a nucleosome at 2.70 Å, Chd1 double-bound to the nucleosome at 2.78 Å and the overall reconstruction (mix of

[Page 14]

particles where Chd1 is single-bound and double-bound to the nucleosome) at 2.60 Å. This approach also yielded a 2.22 Å reconstruction of GroEL, which we used for more precisely defining pixel size. Subsequently, we used MotionCor2 implemented in Relion 5.0 to perform motion correction. The corrected images were imported into CryoSPARC, and 15,463,267 were picked using ‘Blob picker’ (Extended Data Fig. 8). The data were then processed following the same approach, with ‘Heterogeneous Refinement’ for 3D classification, and ‘Non-Uniform Refinement’ for data refinement, yielding a 2.67 Å reconstruction of the mixed dataset containing single- and double-bound nucleosomes. These particles were further processed in Relion using ‘Bayesian Polishing’, yielding 2.44 Å after one round of polishing and 2.37 Å following the second round, with 710,376 particles. This mixed dataset was then subclassified into a 2.54 Å single-bound and 2.52 Å double-bound subsets. The single-bound subset was processed, and the highest-quality particles were selected, yielding a final 2.54 Å reconstruction with 297,046 particles. The double-bound subset was analyzed with three goals in mind: (1) to determine the best resolved, homogeneous dataset; (2) to determine the best connectivity in the Chd1 920-956 region; and (3) to determine a subset of particles that contained a clear DNA-binding domain. This analysis resulted in (1) 2.61 Å reconstruction from 155,592 particles, (2) 2.9 Å reconstruction with well-resolved 920–956 region from 35,257 particles, and (3) a 2.88 Å reconstruction with well-resolved Chd1 DNA-binding domain from 35,158 particles. The final resolutions are reported using the Fourier shell correlation (FSC) at 0.143 cutoff following gold-standard refinement55. Cryo-EM collection, refinement and model building statistics are given in Table 1.

Model building As the reference model, we used the published 2.3 Å nucleotide-free structure of S. cerevisiae Chd1 bound to a nucleosome (Protein Data Bank (PDB) 7TN2 (ref. 3)). The model was rigid body fit into density maps from this study and optimized locally using UCSF ChimeraX56 ‘Fit in map’ option. Following this, Coot57 was used to analyze, optimize and to build the unresolved parts of the model. The models were then refined using Phenix (phenix.real_space_refine) with secondary structure, rotamer and Ramachandran restraints58. The refined models were validated using MolProbity59 and visually in Coot. The figures for the models and cryo-EM reconstructions were generated using UCSF ChimeraX60 and PyMOL (version 3.0 Schrödinger, LLC). UCSF ChimeraX was created by the Resource for Biocomputing, Visualization, and Informatics at the University of California, San Francisco. The development of this software was supported by the National Institutes of Health R01-GM129325 and the Office of Cyber Infrastructure and Computational Biology, National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases.

Statistics and reproducibility No statistical methods were used to predetermine sample sizes and experiments were not randomized. The data distribution was assumed to be normal, but this was not formally tested. For biochemical experiments, the data were excluded if very poorly resolved in the gel lane. The differences between gel-based samples were evaluated with two-tailed Welch’s t-tests, with explicit P values given in source data files. The range of sample sizes is given in the body of each figure or legend. Cryo-EM refinement was performed using the gold-standard approach, with resolutions reported at FSC of 0.143. The data collection and analysis were not performed blind to the conditions of the experiments.

Life sciences study design All studies must disclose on these points even when the disclosure is negative. Sample size

Sample sizes were not predetermined using statistical methods. For cryo-EM data, sufficient numbers of images were collected for nearatomic structure determination. For biochemical experiments (nucleosome sliding, decentering, binding, DNA distortion, cross-linking), each experiment type was performed in triplicate or quadtruplicate. Fitting nucleosome sliding data to single exponentials used 9-13 points, and fitting binding data to a 2-Kd isotherm used 12 points.

Data exclusions

For cryo-EM datasets, no particles were initially excluded, though subclassification identified subsets of particles for different map calculations, as described in the Methods and Extended Data Figure 8. No data were excluded from biochemical experiments.

Replication

Decentering experiments were independently repeated 4-20 times; Sliding experiments were repeated 4-11 times; DNA distortion (entry) experiments were repeated 3-20 times; DNA distortion (dyad) experiments were repeated 4-12 (AMP-PNP) or 2-8 times (ADP•BeF3-); Binding experiments were repeated 3-8 times; V721C cross-linking was performed 3 times.

Randomization

Randomization was performed for "Gold-standard FSC", where the 3D refinement algorithms randomized particle assignments into two sets of equal sizes. In all other experiments, samples were not divided into separate groups and randomization was not appropriate.

Blinding

Blinding was not employed as it is not common for the types of experiments reported in this study.
